subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The screening effect of $d$ -electrons is
questions.solution: The screening effect of \(d\) - electrons is less than \(p\) - electrons. In general, \(d\) and \(f\) electrons have a poor shielding effect compared to \(s\) and \(p\) electrons. This is because \(\mathrm{s}\) and \(\mathrm{p}\) electrons are close to the nucleus whereas \(\mathrm{d}\) and \(\mathrm{f}\) electrons are more diffused (away from the nucleus).
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Assertion : $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{Se}$ is less acidic than $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}$.Reason : $S$ is less electronegative than Se.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: $\mathrm{S}$ is more electronegative than Se.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The atomic radii of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Li</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Na</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Si</mi></math> follow the order:
questions.solution:
Atomic radii increases down the group due to increase in number of shells and decreases along the period due to increase in nuclear charge.

So, atomic radii follows the order:

Na>Li>Si>F
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Let electronegativity, ionisation energy and electron affinity be represented as EN, IP and EA respectively. Which one of the following equation is correct according to Mulliken?
questions.solution: Mulliken suggested that EN of an element is the average of its IP and EA.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The bond length (pm) of $\mathrm{F}_2, \mathrm{H}_2, \mathrm{Cl}_2$ and $\mathrm{I}_2$, respectively is
questions.solution: As the size of halogen atom increase, the bond length $X-X$ bond in $X_2$ molecule increase from $\mathrm{F}_2$ to $\mathrm{I}_2$ hence, the correct answer is (2).
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The catenation tendency of $\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{Si}$ and $\mathrm{Ge}$ is in the order $\mathrm{Ge} < \mathrm{Si} < \mathrm{C}$. The bond energies (in $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{Si}-\mathrm{Si}$ and $\mathrm{Ge}-\mathrm{Ge}$ bonds are respectively ;
questions.solution: The linking of identical atoms with each other to form long chains is called catenation. However, this property decreases from carbon to lead. Decrease of this property is associated with $\mathrm{M}-\mathrm{M}$ bond energy which decreases from carbon to lead.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: To which group and period does the element belong if the electronic configuration of an element in its -2 oxidation state is $1 s^2 2 s^2 2 p^6 3 s^2 3 p^6 ?$
questions.solution:
Electronic configuration $=1 s^2, 2 s^2, 2 p^6, 3 s^2, 3 p^6$ $=16$ electrons
Sulphur $(Z=16)$ refer to group 16 and period 3 .
Sulphur show -2 oxidation state.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The characteristics of elements <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math> with atomic numbers, respectively, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>33</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>53</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>83</mn></math> are:
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> are metalloids and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math> is a metal

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> is a metalloid, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> is a non-metal and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math> is a metal.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math> are metals.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math> are non-metals and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> is a metalloid
questions.solution:
X=As33→ Metalloid

Y=I53→ Non-metal

Z=Bi83→ Metal
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The radius of $\mathrm{La}^{3+}$ (Atomic number of $\mathrm{La}=57$ ) is $1.06 Å$. Which one of the following given values will be closest to the radius of $\mathrm{Lu}^{3+}$ (Atomic number of $\mathrm{Lu}=71$ ) ?
questions.solution:
Ionic radii $\propto \frac{1}{z}$
Thus, $\frac{z_{2}}{z_{1}} \Rightarrow \frac{1.06}{\left(\text { Ionic radii of } \mathrm{Lu}^{3+}\right)}=\frac{71}{57}$
$\Rightarrow$ Ionic radii of $\mathrm{Lu}^{3+}=0.85 Å$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q:
The first ΔiH1 and second ΔiH2 ionization enthalpies (in kJ mol-1 ) and the electron gain enthalpy ΔegH (in kJ mol-1 ) of the elements I, II, III, IV and V are given below
Element ΔiH1 ΔiH2 ΔegH
I 520 7300 − 60
II 419 3051 −48
III 1681 3374 −328
IV 1008 1846 −295
V 2372 5251 +48

the least reactive non- metal and the most reactive metal of these are respectively
questions.solution: Generally metals having low I.E.are more reactive and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi>eg</mi></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>+</mo><mi>ve</mi></math> for noble gas.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The incorrect statement among the following is
options: The first ionization potential of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Al</mi></math> is less than the first ionization potential of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Mg</mi></math>.

The second ionization potential of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Mg</mi></math> is greater than the second ionization potential of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Na</mi></math>.

The first ionization potential of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Na</mi></math> is less than the first ionization potential of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Mg</mi></math>.

The third ionization potential of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Mg</mi></math> is greater than the third ionization potential of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Al</mi></math>.
questions.solution: The second ionization energy, i.e., <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>IE</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Mg</mi></math> is lower than that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Na</mi></math> because in case of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Mg</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> - electron has to be removed whereas in case of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Na</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math>, an electron is removed from the stable inert gas configuration which is difficult.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.q: Correct order of radius of the first orbit of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>He</mi><mo>+</mo></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>Li</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>Be</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> is:
questions.solution:
rαn2Z, as Z increases, the radius of 1st orbit decreases.

Alternatively, higher charge lower radii in the same period or number of proton increases, the size decreases.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The atomic radius of gallium is less than that of aluminium. This is due to
options: Greater shielding power of s-electrons of gallium atom
poor shielding power of s-electrons of gallium
Poor shielding power of d-electrons of gallium
Greater shielding power of d-electrons of gallium
questions.solution: Due to poor shielding effect of d-electrons of gailium, it leads to increase in effective nuclear charge which inturn leads to decrease in the radius of gallium as compared to aluminium.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q:
Among $\mathrm{Me}_{3}\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{C}_{5} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{N}$ and MeCN
(Me $=$ methyl group) the electronegativity of $\mathrm{N}$ is in the order
questions.solution: $\because$ In Me $_{3} \mathrm{N} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}$ is $s p^{3}$ -hybrid and in $\mathrm{MeCN} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}$ is $s p$ -hybrid Also electronegativity of any element $\alpha \%$ $s$ -character in hybrid orbitals. Thus, order of electronegativity for nitrogen is $$ \mathrm{MeCN}>\mathrm{C}_{5} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{N} < \mathrm{Me}_{3} \mathrm{N} $$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Which of the following order is incorrect -
options: <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>–</mo></mrow></msup><mo>⇒</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>
questions.solution: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> is correct order of oxidizing property, as oxidizing property increases in a period due to increase in the electron accepting tendency of the element.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The stability of +1 oxidation state increases in the sequence
questions.solution:
Key Idea Stability of lower oxidation states increases on moving down a group due to inert pair effect.
The given elements belong to third group. These elements mainly exhibit +3 and +1 oxidation state. As we know the stability of lower oxidation state, $i e,+1$ state, increases on moving down a group, the sequence of stability is
$\mathrm{Al} < \mathrm{Ga} < \mathrm{In} < \mathrm{Tl}$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Four successive members of the first two transition elements are listed below with their atomic numbers. Which one of them is expected to have the highest third ionisation enthalpy?
questions.solution: $\mathrm{V}^{2+}(21)[\mathrm{Ar}]^{18} 3 d^3 4 s^0$ $\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}$ (22) $[\mathrm{Ar}]^{18} 3 d^4 4 s^0$ $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ (23) $[\mathrm{Ar}]^{18} 3 d^5 4 s^0$ $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(24) \quad[\mathrm{Ar}]^{18} 3 d^5 4 s^1$ The value of the third ionization enthalpy will be the largest in the case of manganese as the electrons in the orbitals are in half-filled stable electronic configuration and a very large amount of energy is required to overcome this effect.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Which of the following is the most basic oxide?
questions.solution: Dxygen is common in all oxides of group 16, and electronegativity decreases as we down the group thus basic nature of oxide increase. Herse PoO is the most basic oxide.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The correct order of decreasing acidic nature of oxides
questions.solution:
A binary compound of oxygen with another element is called oxide

Oxides can be simple (e.g., MgO, Al2O3 ) or mixed (Pb3O4 ,Fe3O4 ). Simple oxides can be classified on the basis of their acidic, basic or amphoteric character. An oxide that combines with water to give an acid is termed acidic oxide (e.g., SO2 ,Cl2O7,CO2 , N2O5 ). For example, SO2 combines with water to give H2SO3 , an acid.

 The oxides which give a base with water are known as basic oxides (e.g., Na2O, CaO, BaO). For example, CaO combines with water to give Ca(OH)2, a base.

Some metallic oxides exhibit a dual behaviour. They show characteristics of both acidic as well as basic oxides. Such oxides are known as amphoteric oxides. They react with acids as well as alkalies. For example, Al2O3 reacts with acids as well as alkalies.

There are some oxides which are neither acidic nor basic. Such oxides are known as neutral oxides. Examples of neutral oxides are CO, NO and N2O.

  Group 1 Group 2 Group 13 Group 14 Group 15
Period 2 Li Be B C N

According to periodic trend, the electronegativity of the period 2 elements increases in the order :

Li < Be < B < C < N

Electronegativty of the  central atom of the oxide is direclty proportional acidic nature of the oxide.

Therefore, the acidic character of their oxides decreases in the order as follows :

N2O3 > CO2 > B2O3 > BeO > Li2O
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
